title: TimeXer: Empowering Transformers for Time Series Forecasting with Exogenous Variables
authors: Yuxuan Wang*, Haixu Wu*, Jiaxiang Dong, Guo Qin, Haoran Zhang, Yong Liu, Yunzhong Qiu, Jianmin Wang, Mingsheng Long (School of Software, BNRist, Tsinghua University). *Equal contribution.
venue: NeurIPS 2024 (arXiv:2402.19072v4, submitted Feb 2024, last revised Nov 2024)
tokenization_method: ASYMMETRIC, ROLE-DEPENDENT TOKENIZATION with a learnable GLOBAL BRIDGE TOKEN. TimeXer splits the feature set into ONE endogenous (target) series and C exogenous (covariate) series, and tokenizes the two roles at DIFFERENT granularities inside a single canonical Transformer encoder (no block modifications). (1) ENDOGENOUS = PATCH tokens: the target series x in R^T is cut into N = floor(T/P) non-overlapping length-P patches; each patch + a position embedding is linearly projected to a D-dim temporal token (PatchTST-style). (2) ENDOGENOUS GLOBAL token: ONE extra learnable D-dim token per endogenous series (ViT [CLS]-style) that serves as a macroscopic summary and as the BRIDGE to exogenous information. (3) EXOGENOUS = VARIATE tokens (iTransformer-style): each entire exogenous series z^(i) in R^{Tex} is linearly projected (VariateEmbed: R^{Tex}->R^D) to ONE variate token, giving C exogenous tokens. Two attentions then run per block: ENDOGENOUS SELF-ATTENTION over the concatenated [N patch tokens, 1 global token] (size (N+1)x(N+1)) captures intra-series temporal dependencies AND, asymmetrically, Patch-to-Global (global aggregates patches) and Global-to-Patch (patches read the global summary); and EXOGENOUS-TO-ENDOGENOUS CROSS-ATTENTION (size 1xC) where the global token is the QUERY and the C exogenous variate tokens are KEY/VALUE, so external information is injected ONLY through the single global token and then propagated to the patches via self-attention. Output = linear projection over the final [patch, global] endogenous embeddings; exogenous variables are never forecast.
feature_types_handled: numerical / temporal ONLY, split by ROLE (endogenous target vs exogenous covariate). All inputs are real-valued time series. There is NO categorical pathway, NO high-cardinality field handling, NO learned-embedding-per-category, and NO dedicated static-attribute pathway. The genuinely novel coverage is HETEROGENEITY OF TIME-VARYING SUPPORT: because each exogenous series collapses to ONE variate token (a whole-series linear projection), TimeXer tolerates exogenous series that are temporally MISALIGNED with the target, MISSING values, sampled at a DIFFERENT FREQUENCY, or of a DIFFERENT LOOK-BACK LENGTH (Tex != T) — none of which require timestamp alignment because no patch-level/time-aligned operation touches the exogenous side.
token_granularity: MIXED granularity by feature role: (a) endogenous target -> multiple PER-PATCH tokens (one token = one length-P temporal segment); (b) ONE learnable PER-SERIES global token for the endogenous series; (c) each exogenous covariate -> ONE PER-VARIATE token (whole series collapsed to a single D-dim vector). So a single feature is represented at the granularity matching its role: fine (patch) for the thing being predicted, coarse (variate) for the things providing context. Sequence length seen by endogenous self-attention = N+1 (patches+global), INDEPENDENT of C; cross-attention is 1xC, LINEAR in the number of exogenous features.
numerical_strategy: Two raw-scalar-to-embedding linear maps, no per-value transform: (1) endogenous patches via a trainable linear PatchEmbed over each length-P window (+ learned position embedding); (2) exogenous series via a trainable linear VariateEmbed over the whole length-Tex window. NO piecewise-linear, NO periodic/Fourier, NO quantile/target binning, NO per-scalar embedding. Series are normalized (RevIN-style instance normalization is standard in this lineage / tslib) before embedding. Temporal richness for the target comes from PATCHING + attention; for exogenous covariates the whole series is summarized by a single linear projection (coarse, like iTransformer's variate token).
temporal_handling: ASYMMETRIC. Endogenous target: explicit temporal modeling via non-overlapping PATCHING + self-attention over (N+1) tokens (+ learned positional embeddings on patches) — this is PatchTST-style fine temporal structure. Exogenous covariates: NO explicit temporal modeling — each is collapsed to one variate token by a whole-series linear projection, deliberately discarding fine temporal structure so the model can tolerate misalignment/missingness/frequency-mismatch and avoid the cost/noise of patch-level exogenous tokens. Cross-variate temporal lags between exogenous and endogenous are NOT modeled at patch granularity; the only exo->endo pathway is the 1xC cross-attention into the single global token. No recurrence.
scales_to_many_features: Scales LINEARLY in the number of EXOGENOUS variables C, which is the design's headline efficiency claim. Because exogenous variables are embedded ONCE into variate tokens (shared across all L layers) and interact with the target only through a 1xC cross-attention against the single global token, TimeXer AVOIDS the O((1+C)^2) all-pairs variate self-attention that iTransformer pays. Measured on ECL with 320 exogenous variables, TimeXer has a LOWER memory footprint than iTransformer (Figure 5 right). A large-scale stress test uses 3,850 endogenous weather stations each with 36 exogenous meteorological variables (different sampling frequency) and beats iTransformer/PatchTST/DLinear/RLinear. CAVEAT for THIS topic: this is cheap precisely because exogenous variables do NOT interact with EACH OTHER — they only feed the target's global token. For a symmetric many-feature setting where all 70+ features matter mutually, you would either run TimeXer's PARALLEL MULTIVARIATE mode (channel-independence: each of the M variates is treated as endogenous in turn with the rest as exogenous, i.e. M parallel passes with shared weights) — which restores per-variate temporal modeling but costs M passes — or accept that cross-feature interaction is funneled through one global token per target.
downstream_model: Canonical Transformer ENCODER, reused verbatim with no architectural modification (multi-head self-attention + cross-attention + position-wise FFN + LayerNorm + residuals); L blocks, hidden dim D. Long-term config: patch length 16, look-back 96. Short-term EPF config: patch length 24, input 168, horizon 24. Encoder-only forecaster with a linear projection head.
pretraining_objective: none — fully supervised forecasting with L2/MSE loss on the horizon only (exogenous variables are never predicted). No self-supervised pretext task in this paper.
key_results: SOTA on twelve benchmarks. (1) SHORT-TERM forecasting WITH EXOGENOUS variables on EPF electricity-price (5 markets NP/PJM/BE/FR/DE, input 168, horizon 24, each with 2 exogenous covariates): TimeXer best on all five; avg MSE 0.307 / MAE 0.265, vs PatchTST 0.330/0.282, iTransformer 0.335/0.289, Crossformer 0.354/0.284, TiDE 0.412/0.338 (TiDE is an exogenous-specialized baseline), DLinear 0.366/0.314. (2) LONG-TERM MULTIVARIATE (look-back 96, horizons {96,192,336,720} averaged), via channel-independence: ECL 0.171/0.270 (best; iTransformer 0.178, PatchTST 0.205), Weather 0.241/0.271 (best), ETTh1 0.437 (best), ETTh2 0.367 (best), ETTm1 0.382 (PatchTST 0.387, ~tie/slight win), ETTm2 0.274 (best), Traffic 0.466/0.287 — Traffic is the ONE LOSS: iTransformer wins (0.428) because Traffic has 862 mutually-interacting variates where full variate-self-attention helps. (3) LARGE-SCALE: 3,850-station weather with 36 mismatched-frequency exogenous vars, TimeXer MSE 0.200 beats iTransformer 0.207, PatchTST 0.216, DLinear/RLinear. (4) MISSING VALUES: replacing all exogenous series with zeros (0.330) or random (0.333) only mildly degrades vs full TimeXer (0.307) — the two-attention split lets endogenous temporal representation dominate; but corrupting the ENDOGENOUS series collapses performance (MSE ~1.1+). (5) LONGER LOOK-BACK helps, and increasing the ENDOGENOUS look-back helps more than increasing the exogenous look-back. (6) EFFICIENCY: lower memory than iTransformer at C=320 on ECL.
baselines_compared: iTransformer, PatchTST, Crossformer, Autoformer (Transformer family); TimesNet, SCINet (CNN); RLinear, DLinear, TiDE (linear; TiDE specifically designed for exogenous variables). Also positioned against TFT and NBEATSx as prior exogenous-aware deep forecasters, and against classical ARIMAX/SARIMAX.
transferable_idea: ROLE-ASYMMETRIC TOKENIZATION + a single LEARNABLE GLOBAL BRIDGE TOKEN that funnels many context features into a target via cheap 1xC cross-attention. Two reusable pieces for a 70+ heterogeneous-feature tokenizer: (1) DON'T tokenize every feature at the same granularity — give the feature(s) you actually care about fine (patch/per-value) tokens and collapse the many context features to ONE coarse token each, then connect them with cross-attention instead of one giant joint self-attention. This makes cost LINEAR in the number of context features and is exactly the lever needed to keep attention affordable at 70+ features. (2) A [CLS]-style learnable GLOBAL TOKEN as an aggregation hub: many heterogeneous feature tokens (numeric variate tokens, and — by extension — categorical/static tokens produced by type-appropriate encoders) can all feed a small set of global tokens via cross-attention, decoupling per-feature representation from the quadratic interaction cost. The whole-series VARIATE token also gives a clean recipe for ingesting features with MISALIGNED / MISSING / DIFFERENT-FREQUENCY support without timestamp alignment — useful when 70+ features arrive at different cadences.
limitations: No explicit Limitations section (only the NeurIPS checklist references it). Inferred/observed: (1) EXOGENOUS COVARIATES DO NOT INTERACT WITH EACH OTHER — they only feed the endogenous global token via 1xC cross-attention; mutual exogenous interactions and fine exo-to-endo TEMPORAL LAGS are not modeled at patch granularity. The cheapness comes precisely from this restriction. (2) Coarse exogenous token: a whole-series linear projection discards exogenous temporal structure (acceptable for context, but it is NOT a fine temporal encoder for those features). (3) Asymmetry assumes a clear target/covariate split; for a SYMMETRIC many-feature regime it falls back to channel-independent PARALLEL multivariate mode = M passes (one per variate-as-endogenous), so the linear-in-C efficiency story is about the EXOGENOUS setting, not a single joint model over 70+ co-equal features. (4) Loses to iTransformer on Traffic (862 strongly-interacting variates) — when ALL features mutually matter, funneling them through one global token underperforms full variate self-attention. (5) No categorical/high-cardinality/static-attribute pathway.
pitfalls: 1) SCALING CLAIM IS ROLE-SPECIFIC: 'scales to many variables / lower memory than iTransformer' holds because exogenous features get ONE shared variate token and a 1xC cross-attention, NOT full pairwise attention. Do NOT cite TimeXer as 'cheap joint attention over 70+ co-equal features' — that is the multivariate mode, which is M channel-independent passes. 2) HETEROGENEITY OVERCLAIM: 'heterogeneous / irregular variables' here means time-varying numeric series with misalignment/missingness/frequency mismatch — NOT mixed numeric+categorical+static. Borrow the role-asymmetric token + global-bridge pattern, but TimeXer provides NO categorical/static tokenizer; that pathway must be designed and validated separately. 3) MISSING-VALUE ROBUSTNESS is asymmetric and could mislead: the model is robust to garbage EXOGENOUS series only because endogenous temporal patches dominate predictions — this is partly because exogenous lift is modest on these benchmarks, not evidence that the cross-attention deeply uses noisy covariates; corrupting the endogenous series is catastrophic. 4) Many wins are NARROW (ETTm1 ~tie with PatchTST; Weather/ECL margins are small) and the multivariate gains ride on the same RevIN-normalization + patching backbone as PatchTST/iTransformer — the role-asymmetry contribution is isolated only in the EPF exogenous task and the ablation (Table 4), where removing the global token / replacing cross-attention with add/concat does degrade results (avg MSE 0.307 -> 0.316 remove / 0.329 add / 0.312 concat). 5) Single global token per target is a representational bottleneck for the exogenous->endogenous channel; for 70+ richly-interacting context features a single bridge token may underfit (consistent with the Traffic loss).
relevance_to_topic: High-value for the temporal-static-fusion and scaling-interaction concept layers: it is the cleanest published recipe for ingesting MANY context features into a target at LINEAR cost via a global bridge token + cross-attention, and for tolerating features with misaligned/missing/different-frequency support — directly applicable to the time-varying half of a 70+ mixed-feature set, and a template for funneling heterogeneous feature tokens through learnable hub tokens rather than one quadratic joint attention.
code_available: yes — official PyTorch repo https://github.com/thuml/TimeXer (THUML; integrated into the Time-Series-Library/tslib, with scripts/configs for all reported datasets).
